abstract: AI agents deployed as persistent assistants must maintain correct beliefs as their information environment evolves. In practice, evidence is scattered across heterogeneous sources that often contradict one another, new information can invalidate earlier conclusions, and user preferences surface through corrections rather than explicit instructions. Existing benchmarks largely assume static, single-authority settings and do not evaluate whether agents can keep up with this complexity. We introduce ClawArena, a benchmark for evaluating AI agents in evolving information environments. Each scenario maintains a complete hidden ground truth while exposing the agent only to noisy, partial, and sometimes contradictory traces across multi-channel sessions, workspace files, and staged updates. Evaluation is organized around three coupled challenges: multi-source conflict reasoning, dynamic belief revision, and implicit personalization, whose interactions yield a 14-category question taxonomy. Two question formats, multi-choice (set-selection) and shell-based executable checks, test both reasoning and workspace grounding. The current release contains 64 scenarios across 8 professional domains, totaling 1{,}879 evaluation rounds and 365 dynamic updates. Experiments on five agent frameworks and five language models show that both model capability (15.4% range) and framework design (9.2%) substantially affect performance, that self-evolving skill frameworks can partially close model-capability gaps, and that belief revision difficulty is determined by update design strategy rather than the mere presence of updates. Code is available at this https URL.

citations.fullCitation: Carlos E. Jimenez, John Yang, Alexander Wettig, Shunyu Yao, Kexin Pei, Ofir Press, and Karthik Narasimhan. Swe-bench: Can language models resolve real-world github issues? In International Conference on Learning Representations, 2024. URL https://openreview.net/forum?id=VTF8yNQM66.
citations.justification: This paper introduces a key task-oriented benchmark that ClawArena contrasts itself with. The authors cite SWE-bench as an example of an existing evaluation that uses a single-authority environment, highlighting the gap ClawArena fills by introducing multi-source conflicts and dynamic updates.
citations.title: Swe-bench: Can language models resolve real-world github issues?

citations.fullCitation: Xiao Liu, Hao Yu, Hanchen Zhang, Yifan Xu, Xuanyu Lei, Hanyu Lai, Yu Gu, Hangliang Ding, Kaiwen Men, Kejuan Yang, et al. Agentbench: Evaluating llms as agents. arXiv preprint arXiv:2308.03688, 2024. URL https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.03688.
citations.justification: AgentBench is a foundational benchmark for LLM agents that helps contextualize the contribution of ClawArena. The paper positions ClawArena as a necessary next step, moving beyond the static, single-source tasks common in benchmarks like AgentBench to evaluate performance in more realistic, evolving information environments.
citations.title: Agentbench: Evaluating llms as agents

citations.fullCitation: Jian Xie, Kai Zhang, Jiangjie Chen, Renze Lou, and Yu Su. Adaptive chameleon or stubborn sloth: Revealing the behavior of large language models in knowledge conflicts. arXiv preprint arXiv:2305.13300, 2024a. URL https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.13300.
citations.justification: This work, also known as ConflictQA, is highly relevant as it directly investigates how LLMs handle conflicting claims, a core theme of ClawArena. The paper cites this work to demonstrate that while static conflicts have been studied, ClawArena's key innovation is adding dynamic belief revision, where agents must update conclusions as new evidence arrives.
citations.title: Adaptive chameleon or stubborn sloth: Revealing the behavior of large language models in knowledge conflicts

citations.fullCitation: Grégoire Mialon, Roberto Dessı, María Lomeli, Julen Etxaniz, Liam Magee, Federico Belotti, Philip Quirke, Antoine Soubrier Lopez, Luca Beurer-Kellner, Riccardo Pucella, et al. Gaia: A benchmark for general ai assistants. arXiv preprint arXiv:2311.12983, 2024. URL https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.12983.
citations.justification: GAIA represents the state-of-the-art for evaluating general-purpose AI assistants, which is the target agent type for ClawArena. The paper cites GAIA to differentiate its own specific focus on belief maintenance amid conflicting and evolving evidence from other benchmarks that test a broader, but less dynamic, set of agent capabilities.
citations.title: Gaia: A benchmark for general ai assistants

intermediateReport:
The following report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "ClawArena: Benchmarking AI Agents in Evolving Information Environments."

---

### Research Report: ClawArena: Benchmarking AI Agents in Evolving Information Environments

**1. Authors and Institution(s)**

The authors of this preprint are:
*   Haonian Ji, Kaiwen Xiong, Siwei Han, Peng Xia, Shi Qiu, Yiyang Zhou, Jiaqi Liu, Jinlong Li, Bingzhou Li, and Huaxiu Yao, affiliated with UNC-Chapel Hill.
*   Zeyu Zheng, affiliated with the University of California, Berkeley.
*   Cihang Xie, affiliated with the University of California, Santa Cruz.

**2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape**

The work presented in "ClawArena" addresses a recognized gap in the evaluation of AI agents, particularly those designed to function as persistent assistants. Existing benchmarks for AI agents typically operate under simplified assumptions that do not fully reflect the complexities of real-world information environments.

Current task-oriented agent benchmarks, such as SWE-bench, AgentBench, WebArena, OSWorld, and GAIA, primarily focus on evaluating capabilities like tool use, planning, and execution within environments characterized by a single, authoritative source of truth. These benchmarks generally do not assess an agent's ability to resolve conflicts when information diverges across multiple sources.

Similarly, long-context and multi-hop Question Answering (QA) benchmarks, including HotpotQA, MuSiQue, LongBench, and RULER, emphasize information retrieval and composition from static evidence bases. While ConflictQA introduces conflicting claims, the evidence in these settings remains fixed throughout the evaluation, meaning they do not test an agent's capacity to revise prior judgments in response to new, evolving information.

Memory and persona benchmarks, such as LoCoMo and PersonaChat, focus on long-term recall and consistent user interaction. However, these benchmarks typically do not combine the challenges of cross-source conflict resolution, dynamic information updates, and the retention of implicit user preferences within a single evaluation framework.

ClawArena is positioned to bridge these gaps by introducing a benchmark that evaluates AI agents in environments where information is inherently multi-source, dynamic, and user-specific. It aims to assess whether agents can maintain accurate and current beliefs when evidence is fragmented, contradictory, and evolves over time, and when user expectations are conveyed implicitly rather than explicitly. This approach seeks to provide a more comprehensive evaluation of agent performance in deployment scenarios that involve sustained human-in-the-loop interaction.

**3. Key Objectives and Motivation**

The primary objective of the ClawArena benchmark is to evaluate the robustness and reliability of AI agents operating in complex, evolving information environments, specifically those deployed as persistent assistants. The motivation for this benchmark stems from observed limitations in current evaluation methodologies and the inherent challenges agents face in practical, real-world settings.

The authors identify that maintaining a correct understanding of ongoing work is crucial for AI agents serving as persistent assistants. A wrong answer or outdated belief can lead to misinformed decisions in critical areas such as project management, incident response, scheduling, or document generation, even when the correct information might exist within the digital environment. The core difficulty for an agent is not merely retrieving facts but discerning which pieces of evidence should be trusted and how to reconcile conflicting information.

The research outlines three coupled challenges that naturally arise in sustained human-in-the-loop deployments and which form the basis of ClawArena's evaluation dimensions:

1.  **Multi-source conflict reasoning (MS):** Information in real environments is often scattered across heterogeneous sources, such as chat histories, workspace files, and monitoring logs. These sources can frequently contradict one another. An agent must develop the ability to judge the reliability of different sources rather than simply aggregating all claims without discrimination. Existing benchmarks often assume a single authoritative source, failing to assess this critical reasoning capability.
2.  **Dynamic belief revision (DU):** Information environments are not static; new evidence emerges over time that can invalidate previously held conclusions. Agents must therefore be capable of explicitly revising their beliefs and conclusions based on new data, rather than merely accumulating all information in a chronological manner. The ability to self-correct and update understanding is a core requirement for persistent agents.
3.  **Implicit personalization (P):** User preferences are rarely articulated through explicit instructions but instead surface through interaction patterns, corrections, and feedback. An agent must be able to learn and apply these implicit preferences consistently and without constant reminders. Failure to adapt to user-specific styles or formats can result in unreliable or unsatisfactory outputs, even if the core information is accurate.

The motivation is to develop an evaluation framework that integrates these three dimensions, recognizing that they interact non-trivially. An agent that excels in one area but fails in another (e.g., handles conflicts but neglects updates, or revises correctly but ignores user preferences) will still produce unreliable outcomes in practical scenarios. ClawArena is designed to address this holistic problem by simultaneously testing these capabilities in a simulated, yet empirically grounded, evolving environment.

**4. Methodology and Approach**

ClawArena is designed as a benchmark to evaluate AI agents within simulated evolving information environments. The methodology involves a structured scenario design, a multi-dimensional evaluation framework, a specific construction pipeline, and a controlled experimental setup.

**Core Design Principle:**
Each scenario in ClawArena features a hidden ground truth. The agent is exposed only to noisy, partial, and potentially contradictory traces of this truth, delivered through multi-channel session histories, workspace files, and staged updates. Answer correctness is verified against this hidden ground truth, not against any single observable source, ensuring evaluation reliability. Authenticity is maintained by grounding observable materials in real-world empirical distributions of message timing, contact frequency, and information overload.

**Scenario Composition:**
The current release comprises 64 scenarios across 8 professional domains, encompassing 1,879 evaluation rounds and 365 dynamic updates. Each scenario typically includes 5–7 communication channels (e.g., Slack, email, WeChat) with 200–400 messages, and 4–8 workspace documents (e.g., monitoring logs, sprint notes, audit reports). A four-stage personalization protocol, concluding with silent-exam rounds, is also embedded.

**Evaluation Dimensions and Taxonomy:**
ClawArena organizes evaluation around three structural traits of real-world information environments and their corresponding failure modes:
1.  **Multi-source conflict reasoning (MS):** Assesses the agent's ability to judge source reliability when heterogeneous sources contradict each other. Accuracy is measured by an exact-set match for cross-source conflict questions.
2.  **Dynamic belief revision (DU):** Measures the agent's capacity to explicitly revise conclusions when new evidence invalidates previously correct information. The revision rate is tracked.
3.  **Implicit personalization (P):** Evaluates whether the agent can learn and apply user preferences, expressed through corrections and interaction patterns, without explicit reminders. Compliance is scored only in silent-exam rounds.

These three dimensions and their pairwise and three-way interactions form a 14-category question taxonomy, including recall and reasoning variants for each, to prevent systems from achieving high scores by solving only isolated aspects.

**Question Formats:**
Two distinct question formats are used:
1.  **Multi-choice (set-selection):** Agents select the correct subset from 7–9 candidate statements.
2.  **Shell-based executable checks:** These verify whether claims about workspace evidence can be grounded in actual files, testing workspace interaction and grounding.

**Scenario Design Layers:**
Each scenario is constructed with a six-layer specification:
*   **Layer 0 (Hidden Ground Truth):** Defines the objective timeline, contradiction map, and answer provenance. This is never exposed to the agent.
*   **Layers 1-4 (Visible to Agent):** Include workspace files (Layer 1), session histories (Layer 2), evaluation questions (Layer 3), and staged update packages (Layer 4).
*   **Layer 5 (Internal Generation Guide):** Covers formatting constraints and noise controls.

**Specific Design Elements:**
*   **Multi-source conflict design:** Scenarios embed four canonical evidence relations: factual conflicts (C1), authority conflicts (C2), non-conflict slots (C3), and temporal/process conflicts (C4). The non-conflict slot is included to prevent agents from over-flagging every disagreement as a contradiction.
*   **Staged update design:** Updates are injected in phases, introducing incomplete narratives, followed by contradictions, confirmations, or corroboration. Updates can be subjective (appended session messages shifting credibility) or objective (workspace file modifications). Scenarios include anchoring and authority phrases to test self-correction.
*   **Personalization protocol:** Evaluated through four stages: calibration (hints), feedback (corrections), session-implicit (interaction patterns), and silent-exam rounds (scored, no reminders). Preferences cover five dimensions: output format, artifact naming, document structure, analytical style, and communication tone.

**Construction Pipeline:**
A three-stage construction pipeline, combined with empirical distribution constraints, generates scenarios:
1.  **Seed construction:** Initial scenarios were hand-authored and cross-validated to ensure complexity and consistency.
2.  **Meta-specification induction:** Structural invariants (narrative patterns, contradiction ratios, bias insertion, update-question binding) were distilled from the seed scenarios.
3.  **Batch generation with real-world grounding:** Over 200 empirical distributions (e.g., email volume, commit patterns, messaging activity, social network structure) were used to ensure authenticity in workspace documents, session timing, and causal consistency across layers.
4.  **Validation:** Every scenario undergoes three levels of validation: structural, semantic consistency, and control checks, to ensure that failures reflect agent behavior rather than scenario inconsistencies.

**Experimental Setup:**
Experiments were conducted using:
*   **Five AI agent frameworks:** OpenClaw, Claude Code, NanoBot, PicoClaw, and MetaClaw (a skill-driven self-evolving framework built on OpenClaw).
*   **Five language models:** Claude Opus 4.6, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Claude Haiku 4.5, GPT-5.2, and GPT-5.1.
*   **Scoring mechanisms:** Per-option scoring (1 - (fp+fn)/n) for multi-choice in cross-framework, exact-match for cross-model. Graded scoring for belief revision (1 for explicit, 0.5 for acknowledging, 0 for relying on superseded). Automated scripts for personalization in silent-exam rounds. Binary pass/fail for executable checks via sandboxed shell commands.
*   **Evaluation subset:** Due to cost, a 12-scenario subset (337 rounds, 17.9% of full set) was used for cross-model comparisons, selected to cover all 8 domains and ensure representativeness.

**5. Main Findings and Results**

The experiments conducted using ClawArena yielded several findings regarding the performance of AI agent frameworks and language models in evolving information environments.

**Cross-Framework Comparison (GPT-5.1 fixed):**
When the underlying language model was fixed to GPT-5.1, the MetaClaw framework, which incorporates skill-driven self-evolution, achieved the highest Overall score of 0.603. This performance surpassed its base executor, OpenClaw (0.579), by 4.1%. MetaClaw also demonstrated the highest score in Execution Evaluation (0.511), indicating that its skill-based self-evolution mechanism particularly benefited workspace-grounded operations. OpenClaw, however, retained the highest Multi-choice Score (0.854), suggesting that skill injection in MetaClaw might involve a trade-off, slightly reducing pure multi-choice accuracy for enhanced executional robustness. The observed performance range among frameworks, from the lowest (NanoBot and PicoClaw at 0.511) to the highest (MetaClaw), was 0.092, indicating that framework design can substantially influence outcomes, especially when self-evolution capabilities are present. Analysis across different phases of a scenario revealed that MetaClaw's performance peaked in the middle update phases, demonstrating recovery over OpenClaw, while early-phase performance was dominated by in-context reasoning, and terminal-phase reasoning relied more on the underlying executor.

**Cross-Model Comparison (OpenClaw fixed):**
With the framework fixed to OpenClaw, a clear capability gradient was observed across the language models tested: Claude Opus 4.6 (0.735) > Claude Sonnet 4.6 (0.708) > Claude Haiku 4.5 (0.614) > GPT-5.2 (0.581). The model-induced Overall range was 0.154, which is notably wider than the 0.092 framework-induced range. This indicates that the intrinsic capabilities of the base language model play a more dominant role in overall performance across diverse scenarios compared to framework design. Sonnet 4.6 achieved the highest executable-check score (0.489) despite trailing Opus 4.6 in multi-choice scores, suggesting that workspace grounding and abstract reasoning are partially independent capabilities. Furthermore, GPT-5.2 exhibited superior performance in Chinese-language scenarios, despite its lower overall score, indicating the potential influence of language-specific training data. Performance variation across different domains exceeded 60% for every model, highlighting the challenge of domain generalization.

**Error Analysis:**
Detailed error analysis revealed specific patterns of agent failures:
*   **Masking of Failure Modes:** Aggregate scores could conceal qualitative differences in failure modes. For example, two configurations achieving similar high scores on a multi-source reasoning question could fail on structurally opposite options – one by missing a genuine conflict and the other by over-flagging a non-conflict.
*   **Model-Level Bias Propagation:** A consistent narrative anchoring bias was observed across multiple GPT-5.1-based frameworks (excluding Claude Code), where identical incorrect answers were produced on belief revision questions. Claude Code's framework, which emphasized verbatim source quoting, appeared to mitigate this bias, suggesting that framework-level design choices can counteract model-level failure modes.
*   **Belief Revision Difficulty:** The difficulty of belief revision was found to be more dependent on the design strategy of updates rather than merely the volume of updates. Concentrated and targeted contradictory updates led to significant performance drops (0.28–0.36) after the first update, whereas distributed updates across diverse scenarios resulted in only minor overall changes (+1.7% for Haiku). This indicates that benchmark designers can control revision difficulty by focusing on update specificity.
*   **Tool-Chain Bottlenecks:** Executable check performance was largely uncorrelated with multi-choice performance. For instance, in one hospital administration scenario, Haiku achieved 95.2% on multi-choice but 0.0% on executable checks. This discrepancy indicates that capabilities required for workspace grounding, such as file parsing and shell command construction, are distinct from abstract reasoning abilities.
*   **Domain and Language Specificity:** Consistent with the cross-model findings, performance varied significantly across domains for all models. The observed advantage of GPT-5.2 in Chinese-language scenarios reinforces the role of language-specific training data in specialized tasks. The most challenging scenario (a startup scenario with dense updates) yielded low exact-match scores (below 30%) across all tested models, indicating the limits of current agent capabilities in highly complex, dynamic settings.

**6. Significance and Potential Impact**

The introduction of ClawArena represents a contribution to the field of AI agent evaluation by addressing limitations in existing benchmarks. Its significance and potential impact can be summarized as follows:

**Benchmark for Evolving Environments:** ClawArena provides a novel benchmark specifically designed to evaluate AI agents in evolving information environments, a setting that more closely mirrors real-world deployment scenarios for persistent assistants. By focusing on multi-source conflict reasoning, dynamic belief revision, and implicit personalization, it covers critical capabilities often overlooked by benchmarks that assume static, single-authority environments.

**Diagnostic Capabilities:** The benchmark's detailed design, including its 14-category question taxonomy and the separation of hidden ground truth from observable noisy traces, enables a fine-grained diagnostic analysis of agent failures. This allows researchers to pinpoint specific weaknesses across different dimensions and their interactions, moving beyond aggregate scores to understand qualitative failure modes. The inclusion of both multi-choice and shell-based executable checks provides complementary insights into reasoning quality versus practical workspace grounding.

**Insights into Agent Architectures and LLM Capabilities:** The experimental results demonstrate that both the choice of the language model and the design of the agent framework significantly affect performance. The finding that model capability has a larger impact (15.4% range) than framework design (9.2% range) provides empirical evidence for the importance of advancements in foundational models. Concurrently, the observation that self-evolving skill frameworks (like MetaClaw) can partially compensate for underlying model-capability gaps and enhance executional robustness highlights the value of sophisticated agent architectures.

**Understanding Belief Revision:** The research provides insights into the factors governing belief revision difficulty, indicating that concentrated, targeted updates pose a greater challenge than merely a high volume of distributed updates. This finding can inform the design of future benchmarks and agent training strategies for improving adaptability.

**Foundation for Future Research:** ClawArena establishes a foundation for future research in AI agent development. The authors suggest that a natural extension of this work involves moving beyond static files and staged updates towards live, unconstrained environments where agents must formulate their own queries and interact with real-time information sources. This progressive approach could lead to more robust and autonomous AI assistants.

**Practical Implications:** By providing a rigorous evaluation framework for attributes essential for reliable persistent assistants, ClawArena can guide the development of AI agents that are better equipped to handle the complexities of real-world information overload, conflicting data, and evolving user needs. This could lead to more dependable AI systems for critical applications in various professional domains.
overview:
## Introduction: The Challenge of Persistent AI Agents

The rapid advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs) has transitioned AI from simple chatbots to sophisticated agents capable of managing workflows, executing code, and retrieving information. In many real-world applications, these agents are deployed as persistent assistants—systems that interact with users over extended periods, managing multiple information channels like Slack, email, and internal workspaces. However, most current evaluation benchmarks operate under the assumption of a static environment with a single, authoritative source of truth.

In practice, information is rarely static or consistent. A project’s direction might change during a morning meeting, only to be updated again in a private Slack message or a revised spreadsheet. Current benchmarks often fail to capture this complexity, focusing instead on tool use or single-hop retrieval from fixed evidence. ClawArena is a benchmark designed to address this gap by evaluating AI agents in evolving information environments. It tests whether agents can resolve multi-source conflicts, revise their beliefs as new data arrives, and adapt to implicit user preferences without explicit instruction.

![ClawArena Overview](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04202v1/overview.png)
*Figure 1: Overview of ClawArena, illustrating the complex, multi-channel environment where agents must reconcile conflicting and evolving information.*

## The Three Pillars of Evolving Information

The core of ClawArena is built around three structural challenges that arise in sustained human-in-the-loop deployments:

1.  **Multi-source Conflict Reasoning (MS):** In a professional environment, information is scattered across heterogeneous sources such as chat logs, workspace files, and monitoring data. These sources often contradict each other. An agent must determine which source is more reliable—for example, prioritizing a final audit report over an initial informal chat.
2.  **Dynamic Belief Revision (DU):** Real-world environments are not static snapshots. New evidence emerges that can invalidate previous conclusions. An agent must be able to explicitly revise its internal model of the world, rather than simply appending new facts to a growing list of contradictory information.
3.  **Implicit Personalization (P):** Users rarely give a complete list of their preferences at the start of a project. Instead, preferences regarding formatting, tone, and analytical style surface through interactions, feedback, and corrections. Persistent agents must learn these patterns and apply them consistently without being reminded in every prompt.

By integrating these three dimensions, ClawArena provides a holistic evaluation of an agent's ability to maintain a reliable "ground truth" amidst noise and change.

## Methodology: The Six-Layer Scenario Design

To ensure a rigorous evaluation, ClawArena uses a unique multi-layer scenario design. Each scenario is built around a hidden ground truth, referred to as the Narrative Bible or Layer 0 ($L_0$). This layer defines the objective timeline and the definitive answers for all questions. The agent never sees $L_0$; instead, it is exposed only to noisy, partial, and potentially contradictory traces of this truth through subsequent layers.

The layers available to the agent include:
*   **Layer 1 ($L_1$):** Workspace files, such as spreadsheets, code repositories, and logs.
*   **Layer 2 ($L_2$):** Session histories, including multi-channel communications like Slack, email, and IMs.
*   **Layer 3 ($L_3$):** Evaluation questions.
*   **Layer 4 ($L_4$):** Staged update packages that arrive at specific intervals to simulate the passage of time.

This separation ensures that answer correctness is verified against an objective reality ($L_0$), not just against whatever the latest message happens to be. The benchmark includes 64 scenarios across 8 professional domains, such as Hospital Administration, Nonprofit Management, and Tech Startups.

![Pipeline of Construction](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04202v1/pipeline_v2.png)
*Figure 2: The construction pipeline for ClawArena, which leverages real-world statistical distributions to generate authentic, multi-layered scenarios.*

## Designing for Realistic Conflict and Updates

To test an agent's reasoning depth, ClawArena embeds four canonical evidence relations within its scenarios:
*   **Factual Conflict ($C_1$):** Direct contradictions between two sources (e.g., File A says "Blue," Message B says "Red").
*   **Authority Conflict ($C_2$):** Contradictions where one source clearly has more weight (e.g., an official log vs. a rumor).
*   **Non-conflict Slots ($C_3$):** Situations that might appear contradictory but are actually consistent, testing whether agents "over-flag" errors.
*   **Temporal/Process Conflict ($C_4$):** Information that was true at time $t$ but is no longer true at time $t+1$.

The benchmark also utilizes a staged update mechanism. Information is released in phases, introducing incomplete narratives followed by confirmations or contradictions. This allows researchers to track how an agent's performance changes as the environment evolves, specifically measuring the rate at which they successfully "self-correct."

## Evaluation Metrics and Question Taxonomy

ClawArena employs a 14-category question taxonomy that covers recall and reasoning for each of the three main pillars (MS, DU, P) and their intersections. For example, a question might require reasoning about a multi-source conflict ($MS$) that was further complicated by a recent update ($DU$).

![Question Taxonomy](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04202v1/sunburst_v7_notext.png)
*Figure 3: The taxonomy of evaluation rounds in ClawArena, showing the distribution of questions across static, dynamic, and execution-based categories.*

The benchmark uses two primary formats:
1.  **Multi-choice Set-selection:** Agents must select the correct subset from 7–9 candidate statements. Scoring is calculated using a per-option accuracy formula:
    $$1 - \frac{fp + fn}{n}$$
    where $fp$ is false positives, $fn$ is false negatives, and $n$ is the total number of options.
2.  **Executable Checks:** These verify if an agent's claims about workspace data can be grounded in actual files. For instance, if an agent claims to have fixed a bug, a sandboxed shell environment runs a test suite to verify the fix.

## Experimental Findings: Frameworks vs. Models

The benchmark was used to evaluate several state-of-the-art language models and agent frameworks. One of the most significant findings was the capability gradient between models. When the framework was kept constant, the model-induced performance range was $0.154$, whereas the framework-induced range (when the model was kept constant) was only $0.092$. This suggests that the intrinsic reasoning and memory capabilities of the base LLM are currently more influential than the specific architecture of the agent framework.

![Case Analysis 1-2](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04202v1/12.png)
*Figure 4: Analysis of Multi-Source Conflict Reasoning and Dynamic Belief Revision, showing how different frameworks can either mitigate or exacerbate model biases.*

### Key Model Results:
*   **Claude Opus 4.6** achieved the highest overall score of $0.735$.
*   **Claude Sonnet 4.6** followed closely with $0.708$ and performed best on executable checks ($0.489$).
*   **GPT-5.2** scored $0.581$, though it showed a distinct advantage in Chinese-language scenarios, likely due to its training data distribution.

### Framework Performance:
The MetaClaw framework, which features a skill-driven self-evolution mechanism, achieved an overall score of $0.603$, outperforming its base version, OpenClaw ($0.579$), by $4.1\%$. This improvement was particularly notable in execution-based tasks, suggesting that self-evolution helps agents better navigate workspace files and tools.

![Case Analysis 3-4](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04202v1/34.png)
*Figure 5: Case studies on self-diagnostic accuracy and implicit preference compliance.*

## Error Analysis and Persistent Biases

ClawArena’s fine-grained diagnostic capabilities revealed several recurring failure modes in modern AI agents:

**Narrative Anchoring Bias:** Agents often "lock in" on the first narrative they encounter. Even when subsequent updates provided explicit evidence to the contrary, many agents continued to rely on the initial, now-outdated information. This was particularly evident in "belief revision" questions where concentrated, targeted updates led to performance drops of $0.28$ to $0.36$.

**Tool-Chain Bottlenecks:** There was a significant gap between an agent's ability to reason about a problem and its ability to execute a solution. For example, in one hospital administration scenario, a model achieved $95.2\%$ on multi-choice reasoning but $0.0\%$ on the associated executable checks. This discrepancy indicates that grounding reasoning in practical workspace actions remains a major hurdle.

**Temporal Policy Scoping:** Agents struggled with "Norm Retroactivity." If a user introduced a new formatting rule ($P$) in the middle of a project, agents often mistakenly applied that rule to documents created *before* the rule was established, failing to understand the temporal scope of user preferences.

![Case Analysis 5-6](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04202v1/56.png)
*Figure 6: Case studies illustrating the "Declaration-Reality Gap" in executable checks and statistical methodology conflicts.*

## Implications for the Future of AI Development

ClawArena highlights that building a truly persistent AI assistant requires more than just scaling context windows or improving retrieval. It requires agents that can:
*   **Model Source Reliability:** Not all information is equal. Agents must learn to weigh evidence based on its provenance and authority.
*   **Actively Prune Beliefs:** Instead of just accumulating data, agents must proactively discard or "mark" information that has been superseded by new updates.
*   **Integrate Long-term Implicit Memory:** Personalization must be handled as a continuous learning process rather than a one-time instruction.

![Case Analysis 7-8](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04202v1/78.png)
*Figure 7: Final case studies showing the difficulty of full-dimension integration where MS, DU, and P all interact simultaneously.*

By providing a benchmark that mirrors the messiness and evolution of real-world information, ClawArena offers a path forward for developing more reliable, adaptable, and personalized AI agents. As models continue to improve, this framework will serve as a rigorous testing ground for the next generation of autonomous digital assistants.

keyInsights: The intrinsic capabilities of the base language model have a more dominant impact on overall agent performance (0.154 range) compared to the design of the agent framework (0.092 range).

Self-evolving agent frameworks, exemplified by MetaClaw, can improve performance, especially in execution-oriented tasks and during mid-scenario updates, potentially mitigating some underlying model limitations.

Belief revision difficulty is primarily driven by the strategic design of updates, with concentrated and targeted contradictions leading to greater performance drops than merely a high volume of distributed updates.
originalProblem: Existing AI agent benchmarks operate on simplified assumptions, typically using static environments with a single source of truth, failing to mirror real-world complexities.
Agents struggle with resolving conflicting information originating from multiple heterogeneous sources (e.g., chat, files) and discerning source reliability.
Current evaluation methods do not adequately assess an agent's ability to revise prior beliefs in response to new, evolving information or to learn and apply implicit user preferences over time.
results: MetaClaw, a skill-driven self-evolving framework built on OpenClaw, achieved the highest overall score of 0.603 with GPT-5.1, surpassing OpenClaw by 4.1% and demonstrating superior Execution Evaluation.
Language models showed a clear capability gradient with OpenClaw, from Claude Opus 4.6 (0.735) as the top performer, followed by Sonnet 4.6 (0.708), Haiku 4.5 (0.614), and GPT-5.2 (0.581).

Error analysis revealed that workspace grounding capabilities (executable checks) are largely uncorrelated with abstract reasoning (multi-choice scores), indicating distinct challenges, and model-level biases like narrative anchoring can propagate across frameworks.
solution: ClawArena provides a benchmark of 64 scenarios across 8 professional domains, featuring a hidden ground truth and noisy, partial, and potentially contradictory observable information via multi-channel histories and files.
It integrates evaluation across multi-source conflict reasoning, dynamic belief revision, and implicit personalization, using a 14-category question taxonomy to capture diverse failure modes.

Scenarios are constructed with empirical distributions to ensure authenticity and include both multi-choice set-selection questions and shell-based executable checks for comprehensive assessment of abstract reasoning and workspace grounding.
summary.summary: ClawArena introduces a benchmark for evaluating AI agents in complex, dynamic information environments, focusing on multi-source conflict reasoning, dynamic belief revision, and implicit personalization. Experiments indicate that underlying language model capabilities more significantly impact overall performance (0.154 range) than agent framework design, though sophisticated frameworks like MetaClaw can enhance execution-oriented tasks.

title: ClawArena: Benchmarking AI Agents in Evolving Information Environments
